summary: DeVault examines whether private platforms should engage in censorship, weighing freedom of association against freedom of speech. He explores regulatory and antitrust approaches to the problem, finding contradictions in free speech absolutist positions. He argues that democratic and judicial processes are too corrupt, slow, and majoritarian to protect marginalized people from hate speech. Writing as a self-described leftist and pragmatist, he concludes that the current system where market incentives align with moral outcomes on private censorship is producing approximately just results, even if imperfect.
key_points: Freedom of association is just as important as freedom of speech, and expanding free speech for platform users requires curtailing freedoms for platform owners and staff
Free speech absolutists face contradictions: their proposed solutions (regulation, antitrust) conflict with their typical small-government philosophy
Democratic and judicial processes are too corrupt, slow, and majoritarian to adequately protect marginalized people from hate speech
Current market incentives happen to align with moral outcomes on censorship, as businesses lose users and staff by platforming bigots
Inaction on hate speech is a form of complicity that prioritizes abstract principles over the realities faced by marginalized people
money_quotes: Radical free speech for individuals requires radical curtailing of free association for businesses.
Those who describe themselves as Libertarian and make either of these arguments provide me with no small amount of amusement.
Should the rights of trans people to live free of harassment be subject to the pleasure of the cisgendered majority?

Inaction is a form of complicity; allowing hate to remain on your platform is an acknowledgement of your favor towards the lofty principles outlined in the arguments above in spite of the problems enumerated here and the realities faced by marginalized people today.
I reject the idea that it is appropriate for businesses to sideline morality in favor of profit, and I don't have much faith in the free market to produce moral outcomes.
The free market currently rewards private censorship, therefore in a system wherein the free market reigns supreme we observe private censorship.
These systems, when implemented, will perform as they always have: they will provide disproportionately unfavorable outcomes for disadvantaged members of society.
key_insight: Freedom of association is as fundamental as freedom of speech, and under current conditions where market incentives align with protecting marginalized people, private censorship produces approximately just outcomes despite systemic imperfections.
